And alhamdulillah, if we were to gather all the weak narrations of out of about 2,000, just shy of 2,000 narrations is a little less than 70 or at most a few narrations over 70. There's no narrations in which are agreed upon amongst the scholarship of Islam to be weak. And so this narration which many people know the wise, right? So a wise individual, the intelligent, right? He judges himself correctly. He doesn't give himself a bunch of breaks. Doesn't come up with a bunch of excuses to justify his poor behavior. The intelligent person is someone who who judges himself accordingly. And does actions for that which comes after death. He puts things forth for himself. He doesn't make his major actions and his major efforts something that are specific in particular to this world. What he really works towards is what is after death. This all goes back to intelligence. Why? Because the intelligent person what separates us from animals in intelligence. One of the main differences between human beings and animals with respect to intelligence is that the human being is able to imagine the future and is able to tie consequences to the present even if they can't see those consequences immediately. You look at an animal, the only way you can really teach an animal, even really smart animals, is to give them immediate punishment or immediate reward. This is the only way you can build proper behavior. Many times if you punish an animal, it doesn't even know why it's being punished. It has no idea, does not understand. The human being has what is referred to in the Quran as a is able to look and conceive in the future of a consequence that may not occur right now but will occur far into the future. A human being is able to do this. animals, they can't do this. Animals cannot delay gratification unless it's part of their nature. And animals cannot with withhold themselves and fight against their instincts because they have reached to a a conclusion that this is bad for me because of something that will be in the future, not right now. Animals can't do that. Their intelligence is too blunted. And so the human being who behaves like an animal, this is un this is the unintelligent person. an intelligent person. True Kesa, true intelligence and cleverness and wisdom that a person is able to conceive of this having an effect way later down the line even past death. And so the prophet or and so in this narration, the truly wise, the truly clever intelligence, the intelligent one is one who is able to bring himself to account and does things and works deeds for what is coming after death. Because they have deduced that this cannot be the only thing that we exist for. There has to be something after death. >> >> And Muhammad.

summary: The speaker discusses the seventh narration in a chapter about vigilance of Allah's observation from Riyadul Saliheen. The narration, attributed to Abu Shad Ibuad, contains a famous saying of the Prophet Muhammad that many Muslims recognize, though the narration has a weak chain of transmission. The speaker explains the methodology of hadith authentication, noting that the author of Riyadul Saliheen relied heavily on earlier scholars' authentication work. Despite the weak chain, the meaning of this narration is correct and supported throughout the Quran and Sunnah. The speaker emphasizes that weak narrations can still be cited when their meaning aligns with established Islamic principles.
suggested_title: The Seventh Narration on Intelligence and Self-Accountability from Riyadul Saliheen
hadith_chapter: Chapter of Vigilance of Allah's Observation
